result:
I’ve been thinking about the strange comfort of maps—how they promise a clean, folded version of reality, and how we keep inventing new ones even when we know they can’t be trusted.

A map is a claim. It says: here are the edges, here are the roads, here is the boundary between the water and the land, here is the safe path through the mountains, here is where you are. It looks objective because it wears the costume of geometry. North at the top, neat lines, neat labels. Even when it’s decorated with sea monsters and wind-roses, it still carries the quiet authority of measurement. But the more you look, the more you notice what’s missing: smell, slope, noise, fear, time. The map has no way to draw the moment you realize you’re lost, the subtle panic that makes your thoughts run faster than your feet. No contour line can capture the strange way a city street changes at 2 a.m., when the same sidewalk that was ordinary at noon becomes a corridor of caution. The map is a reduction, and yet we keep reaching for it because reduction is how the mind survives.

There’s a kind of innocence in early maps. “Here be dragons” is both a warning and an admission: we don’t know. Modern maps pretend the dragons are gone, but the unknown is still there—just relocated to different layers. It’s in the gaps between official and lived reality. A phone’s navigation app might be able to tell you the fastest route to a café, but it can’t tell you whether the café feels welcoming. It can’t tell you if the air inside is too bright, if the music drills into your skull, if the barista’s indifference will make you feel small. It can’t tell you whether you’ll sit by the window and suddenly remember someone you haven’t spoken to in years. We don’t just travel through space; we travel through mood, memory, and expectation, and those are harder to pin down than longitude.

Still, maps evolve because our needs evolve. We didn’t always map the same things. At one time, mapping meant coastlines and trade routes—how to move goods and ships without dying. Later, it meant railroads, property lines, census blocks, weather systems, electrical grids. Now we map attention. We map the way eyes move across screens. We map the probability that a person will click a button, buy a thing, believe a claim. In a way, the modern world is a contest of cartographers, not of land but of behavior. Whoever draws the most persuasive map of human choice gets to place the signposts.

I think that’s why maps have begun to feel uncanny. When you open a navigation app, you’re not just looking at a map—you’re looking at a map that is looking back. It learns your routines. It predicts where you’ll go before you decide. It offers suggestions that quietly shape your future. You take the “recommended” route, and the recommendation becomes reality; the map doesn’t merely describe the world, it edits it. Enough people follow the same directions and suddenly a quiet neighborhood street becomes a highway of strangers. A detour becomes permanent. A business thrives because a pin on a screen got popular. A city becomes less like itself because a million micro-decisions were coordinated by software that optimized for speed rather than beauty.

There’s an old argument about whether the map is the territory. The territory is messy: mud, weeds, stray dogs, unexpected kindness. The map is clean. But the truth is less philosophical and more practical: sometimes the map becomes the territory. Not in the metaphysical sense, but in the sense that people build their lives around what gets represented. If a neighborhood isn’t on the “good” maps—if it’s missing from tourist guides, absent from glossy listicles, poorly served by transit apps—then it becomes harder for the people there to attract resources, attention, investment. Being unmapped is a kind of poverty. Being mapped badly is another.

And then there are the private maps, the ones we don’t talk about as maps at all. Each person carries a mental atlas made of shortcuts, landmarks, and stories. “Turn left where the old bakery used to be.” “Meet me by the tree that looks like it’s holding up the sky.” “That alley is where I fell in love / got mugged / learned to ride a bike.” We navigate not by coordinates but by meaning. Two people can stand at the same intersection and be in different cities, because their histories fill the space with different weather. If you’ve ever walked into a room where something bad happened and felt your body remember before your mind did, you know what I mean. The floor plan is the same; the map is different.

I’m fascinated by the way language itself is a map. We give names to things to make them portable. “Grief,” for example, is a word we use to point at a thousand variations of loss. Saying it doesn’t capture it, but it gives us a handle. A name is a pin dropped into experience: here, something happened. We can return to it. We can invite someone else to come look at it with us. In that sense, every sentence is a tiny cartographic act: it decides what to include, what to ignore, and what to call the center.

This is why writing can feel like walking through fog with a compass. You start with a vague direction—an image, a question, a sensation—and you take one step at a time, hoping the next patch of ground is solid. The first draft is often a bad map: distorted, missing roads, wrong scale. But it’s still useful because it tells you where you’ve already been. Revision is surveying. You return to the same territory and measure it more honestly. You realize that what you thought was a mountain is actually a hill, or that the river bends somewhere you didn’t expect. You find the places where your earlier version lied, not maliciously, but because it didn’t know better.

The maps we trust most are usually those that admit their limits. A hiking map that marks “seasonal stream,” “unstable slope,” “avalanche risk” is a map that respects the living world. It doesn’t pretend the landscape is static. Likewise, I trust people more when their self-maps include the warning labels: “I get defensive when I feel cornered,” “I disappear when I’m overwhelmed,” “I’m trying to be better at apologizing.” Those aren’t flaws; they’re topographic features. The danger is not having cliffs; it’s pretending the cliffs aren’t there.

There’s also joy in getting lost—real, bodily lost, not the curated “wander” of a travel brochure. Getting lost can be terrifying, but it can also be a reset. It interrupts autopilot. It forces you to notice. You stop moving on assumptions and start moving on evidence: the angle of the sun, the sound of traffic, the smell of the sea. You become a creature again, not just a user following instructions. Some of my best memories are the result of wrong turns: stumbling into a small bookstore, finding a street musician under a bridge, discovering a café that wasn’t rated by anyone because it didn’t need to be. In those moments, you realize that the world is bigger than what has been indexed.

Yet I don’t want to romanticize it. Not everyone can afford to be lost. For some people, being lost is a mild inconvenience; for others, it’s danger. That’s another hidden truth about maps: they’re not neutral. Who gets to explore without fear? Who is welcomed when they arrive? Who is questioned for existing in the “wrong” place? A map doesn’t show power, but power is one of the main forces shaping the terrain. The “safe route” for one person is not the safe route for another. The same street can be a corridor or a barrier depending on who you are and what the world has decided to assume about you.

Maybe that’s why I like the idea of making many maps rather than one. A city map that includes wheelchair accessibility. A night map that includes streetlights and late buses. A community map that includes mutual aid networks and free clinics. A sensory map that includes quiet places, crowded places, places that smell like rain or frying garlic. A memory map that includes where you learned something important. A kindness map: places where strangers helped you when you didn’t ask.

If I had to choose one map to carry, it might be the simplest: a map of questions. Not “Where am I?” but “What do I need right now?” Not “What’s the fastest path?” but “What’s the path that won’t make me regret the day?” Not “What should I do?” but “What am I avoiding?” These questions don’t replace roads and coordinates, but they point to the real navigation problem: most of the time we aren’t trying to get from one place to another. We’re trying to get from one version of ourselves to another—less afraid, more awake, more willing to be changed by what we find.

And maybe that’s the deepest comfort maps offer: the belief that movement is possible. Even a flawed map says, implicitly, that there is a way through. That the unknown can be approached. That you can start where you are, draw a line, and follow it long enough to arrive somewhere new.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 1000 words.
